title: Level 2 — Mixed Contour Quiz
intro: 15 short questions, drawn from across Sections C1 to C6. Numeric and multiple-choice answers are auto-graded; everything else shows the model answer and lets you self-mark. Aim for 12+ correct out of 15 with no family-level errors on feature identification.

skillAreas.label: Feature recognition (summits, valleys, gullies, ridges, spurs)

questions.title: Contour intervals
questions.prompt: The Section C7.1 extract has a 10 m contour interval. If you walk from a contour at 2,180 m to one at 2,260 m, how many contour lines have you crossed and how much height have you gained?
questions.explanation: 2,180 m to 2,260 m is 80 m of climb at 10 m intervals = 8 crossings.

questions.title: Steepness from spacing
questions.prompt: Point Q1 sits on a slope where contours are 2 mm apart. Using the C1.2 rule of thumb (1:25,000 with 10 m contours), is this slope at or above 30 degrees?
questions.explanation: 2 mm spacing sits well inside the 30° threshold (which is around 3 mm on these parameters).

questions.title: Identify the feature (snippet A)
questions.prompt: Snippet **A** on the snippet sheet shows a contour pattern. Name the feature.
questions.modelAnswer: Spur (V-down). The V points downhill; closed loops are absent; contours show a projecting ridge from a parent feature.

questions.title: Identify the feature (snippet B)
questions.prompt: Snippet **B** on the snippet sheet. Name the feature.
questions.modelAnswer: Bowl. U-shape or arc opening downhill, tightly spaced contours at the back (headwall), wider on the flanks.

questions.title: Identify the feature (snippet C)
questions.prompt: Snippet **C** on the snippet sheet. Name the feature.
questions.modelAnswer: Plateau. One closed loop around a broad nearly contour-free area, with a spot height as the only altitude reference.

questions.title: Aspect to eight directions
questions.prompt: On the C7.1 extract, the slope at point Q2 falls towards the lower-right corner of the page. Grid north points up the page. The aspect is:
questions.explanation: Lower-right corner with north-up = SE. If you answered S or E alone, the four-direction default has crept back in — recycle C4.2.

questions.title: Fall line
questions.prompt: At point Q2, sketch the fall-line arrow on the printed extract. The arrow runs perpendicular to the contour at Q2, pointing from higher to lower contour, in the SE direction.
questions.expectations: Arrow drawn perpendicular to the contour (not parallel), pointing downhill in the SE direction.

questions.title: Inverse pairing
questions.prompt: A V-shape of contours points downhill. The feature is:
questions.explanation: V down = spur (or ridge if the feature is long enough — both accepted). If you chose valley or gully, the C2.3 inverse-pairing rule has not stuck.

questions.title: Col vs summit
questions.prompt: In one short sentence, name the contour-pattern difference between a summit and a col.
questions.modelAnswer: A summit shows closed contour loops with the highest contour at the centre; a col shows two opposite sides of contours rising (towards two summits) and two opposite sides falling (towards two valleys). The difference is the contour pattern, not the elevation.

questions.title: Bowl reading
questions.prompt: A bowl shows what contour pattern, and where on that pattern would you expect wind-loaded snow to accumulate?
questions.modelAnswer:
**Pattern:** U-shape or arc of contours opening downhill, with tightly spaced contours at the back (headwall) and wider-spaced contours on the flanks.

**Wind-loaded snow accumulates:** in the upper part of the bowl, just below the headwall, on the lee side of the prevailing wind. Cornices may build on the rim above.

questions.title: Cliff band cues
questions.prompt: Two cues together identify a cliff band on most national alpine 1:25,000 series. Name them.
questions.modelAnswer:
1. **Merged or near-merged contours** drawn as a single thick line.
2. **Rock symbology** overlaid (hatching on IGN, stippling on Swisstopo, rock-face shading on Alpenvereinskarte).

Either cue alone can mislead. Both together are diagnostic.

questions.title: Skinning gradient
questions.prompt: The comfortable skinning gradient band is approximately what range, and what contour spacing on a 1:25,000 map with 10 m contours corresponds to the upper end of that band?
questions.modelAnswer:
**Gradient range:** 8 to 12 degrees comfortable; up to 15 degrees as the upper sustained limit.

**Upper-end contour spacing:** 5 to 6 mm on a 1:25,000 map with 10 m contours (using the C1.2 rule).

Note: the comfortable band (8–12) and the upper limit (up to 15) are different concepts.

questions.title: Terrain trap
questions.prompt: In one sentence, give the simplest definition of a terrain trap that a Level 2 candidate should be able to apply.
questions.modelAnswer: A piece of ground where a small avalanche or fall produces disproportionate consequences, often because the configuration concentrates moving snow (gully), removes the exit (single-mouth bowl), or adds a vertical drop (cliff band below the slope). Terrain trap is about the consequence, not just the slope angle.

questions.title: Sketch — sharp peak with two spurs
questions.prompt: Sketch the side profile of a sharp peak with two spurs running out from it on different aspects. Label the summit and the two spurs.
questions.expectations: Sharp peak (narrow, pointed, steep flanks); two spurs as projections from the summit; labels for summit and both spurs; spurs facing different aspects (a brief direction note for each is helpful).

questions.title: Written description (Q3)
questions.prompt: Pick the point on the C7.1 extract that your trainer marks as Q3. Write a four-sentence description of what you would see standing at Q3, using the four-part structure from C5.3 (underfoot, slope and aspect, near features, far features).
questions.modelAnswer:
A strong answer uses all four parts of the structure, eight-direction aspect terms, C1.2 steepness vocabulary, specific feature references (named summit, valley, col, spur), and describes the imagined view rather than the symbol layout.

_For the standard Q3 (moderate NE-facing slope at ~2,250 m, summit at 2,500 m to the SW, broad valley with stream at 1,950 m to the NE):_

1. **Underfoot:** Firm spring snow with a thin breakable crust on the lee side of small ridges; rocks outcropping above.
2. **Slope and aspect:** A NE-facing slope at around 25 degrees (4 mm contour spacing), opening into a wider bowl below.
3. **Near features:** A small spur to the SE forming the lower edge of a shallow bowl; a stream channel cutting across the valley floor 300 m below.
4. **Far features:** The summit at 2,500 m to the SW, with two visible spurs running NW and S from it; a band of cliffs forming the headwall of the corrie to the west.
